title: Add image digest type to image id for commit
body:
When outputting the newly created image ID from the commit process, prepend
the image digest type for compatibility with other container runtimes.

Resolves: #788

Signed-off-by: baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

comments.body:
> Also, wait, isn't the image's digest a SHA256, while the ID is just a 256-bit random integer?

Why do images have non-digest IDs?  Can we replace random IDs with digests?  That gets you automatic deduping, cryptographically secure identifiers, which sound like pure upside vs. random IDs.

IDs and digests are indeed distinct, per https://github.com/containers/storage/blob/master/images.go#L26-L31

That does not that ID can be specified when the image is created, so it could theoretically be the sha256 digest as well? Don't want to assume that it is, though.

@nalind can probably answer the questions on why ID and digest are distinct better than I.

Regardless, I'm not really comfortable prefixing things with SHA256: if they're not actually SHA256 sums of something.

> Regardless, I'm not really comfortable prefixing things with SHA256: if they're not actually SHA256 sums of something.

I agree, which is why I think we should drop our random IDs and return the digest here instead.  Could this PR get there just by switching to:

```golang
fmt.Println(newImage.Digest())
```

or would that break something else?

The digest is not (AFAICT) usable as input to `podman run` or `inspect` etc. Lots of tool chains do:
```
imgid=$(docker commit cid myimg)
docker run $imgid ...
```
It seems useful to preserve that docker behavior in podman.

imgid=$(docker commit cid myimg) docker run $imgid ...

Looks like Docker uses [much shorter IDs][1].  But if you allow unique abbreviations of local digests (I do n't know if we do), I still don't see the point to a separate ID.

[1]: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/commit/

So, leaving aside the digest vs ID discussion... It's clear that we're returning an ID, which is definitely not a SHA256. As such, I do not think we should prepend the `sha256` string to it, even if that's expected Docker behavior. If we do, people will expect that it is a hash, and be confused when it's not
I agree. @baude ok if I close this PR.
